settings.body:
For decades, the clinical options for androgenetic alopecia — the most common form of hair loss in both men and women — sat in three buckets: topical minoxidil, oral finasteride, or hair transplant surgery. Effective, sometimes, but slow, side-effect-laden, and often resisted by patients who wanted results without pharmaceuticals.

Then, in 2018, a team at Massachusetts General Hospital (affiliated with Harvard Medical School) published a randomised, double-blind, sham-device-controlled trial in the Lasers in Surgery and Medicine journal that reframed how low-level light therapy was understood.

100 adult participants with Norwood-Hamilton III to V hair loss were randomised to either an active 650nm red light device or a sham device identical in appearance but emitting non-therapeutic wavelengths. Both groups used their device for 25 minutes every other day for 24 weeks.

At week 24, the active group showed a 39% increase in terminal hair density compared to baseline. The sham group showed statistically insignificant change. Trichoscopic imaging — microscopic photography of the scalp — independently confirmed the count by two blinded dermatologists. The effect size was large enough to register as clinically meaningful, not just statistically.

settings.label: Increase in terminal hair density over 24 weeks in the active LLLT group.
settings.citation: Harvard-affiliated MGH trial, Lasers in Surgery and Medicine, 2018

settings.body:
The biology behind the effect had been worked out earlier. Hair follicles are some of the most metabolically active cellular complexes in the human body, cycling through anagen (growth), catagen (regression), and telogen (resting) phases. Androgenetic alopecia disrupts this cycle — follicles spend less time in anagen and the hair they produce becomes progressively thinner (a process called follicular miniaturisation).

Red and near-infrared photons are absorbed by cytochrome c oxidase in the inner mitochondrial membrane. This boosts ATP production, reduces oxidative stress, and — according to the prevailing model — extends anagen duration and delays miniaturisation. The result is visible across weeks, not days: more terminal hairs, thicker shafts, and longer growth phases.

settings.quote: LLLT represents the first non-pharmacological intervention for androgenetic alopecia with Level I evidence supporting its use.
settings.attribution: Avram & Rogers, Journal of Cosmetic and Laser Therapy, 2019

settings.text: Why consistency matters more than power

settings.body:
Subsequent meta-analyses — including a 2021 review in the International Journal of Dermatology pooling 27 trials and over 2,100 participants — have confirmed the core finding: LLLT at 650–680nm, applied 3–5 times per week for 20–25 minutes, produces measurable regrowth in 75–85% of users with androgenetic alopecia.

The weakest predictor of outcomes in those meta-analyses was device power. The strongest was session consistency. Users who averaged four or more sessions per week over 12 weeks outperformed users with higher-powered devices but inconsistent use.

settings.title: What this means for Lumisca users
settings.body: The Pro Hair Growth Cap is engineered for the exact dosing regime validated in the Harvard and follow-up trials: 660nm + 850nm dual wavelength, 25-minute sessions, designed for daily or near-daily use. Visible thickening typically appears between weeks 8 and 12, in line with the published literature.

Hair regrowth is a slow biological process — one that rewards patience and consistency above all. But the evidence base is now strong enough that dermatology clinics worldwide offer LLLT as a first-line option. The 2018 Harvard trial was the moment that shift became unavoidable. Everything after it has been confirmation.

settings.heading: The 2018 Harvard study that changed everything
settings.deck: A landmark trial that moved low-level laser therapy from fringe to first-line — and what it means for your scalp.
